query: indications for treating elevated LDL cholesterol in adults aged 45+ with multiple cardiovascular risk factors

retrieved_docs.title: Atorvastatin -- Indications -- Evidence From the Guidelines
retrieved_docs.content: According to the 2018 American Heart Association/American College of Cardiology (AHA/ACC) cholesterol guidelines, patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) should be treated with high-intensity statin therapy to achieve at least a 50% reduction in low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C). If high-intensity statins are not tolerated, the maximally tolerated dose of statin should be used. In individuals at very high risk—defined by a history of multiple major ASCVD events or one major event plus multiple high-risk conditions—more aggressive LDL-C lowering is recommended, with a threshold of less than 70 milligrams per deciliter (mg/dL) or 1.8 millimoles per liter (mmol/L). Adults with severe primary hypercholesterolemia, defined as LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL (≥4.9 mmol/L), should begin high-intensity statin therapy regardless of estimated 10-year ASCVD risk. For adults aged 40 to 75 years with diabetes mellitus and LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL (≥1.8 mmol/L), moderate-intensity statin therapy is indicated. High-intensity statin therapy is reasonable in diabetic individuals at higher risk, such as those aged 50 to 75 years or with multiple risk factors. In adults aged 40 to 75 years without diabetes mellitus but with LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL and an estimated 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5%, moderate-intensity statin therapy is recommended. If the calculated risk is 20% or greater, high-intensity statin therapy should be used to target a reduction of at least 50% in low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperlipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: There are three major high-risk categories when it comes to hyperlipidemia and ASCVD. They are patients with diabetes, those with severe hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dl), and adults aged 40-75 years. Patients of any age with LDL-C ≥ 190 mg/dl should receive treatment with high-intensity statin therapy to reduce LDL-C levels to <100 mg/dl. Ezetimibe is typically added if the goal is not achieved.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercholesterolemia -- Evaluation -- Screening recommendations:
retrieved_docs.content: Men older than 35 Women older than 45 Presence of diabetes Tobacco use Family history of cardiac disease Personal history of heart disease or peripheral vascular disease Obesity (BMI > 30) Hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: HMG-CoA Reductase Inhibitors -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: LDL-C 70 to 189 mg/dL: With diabetes mellitus: Initiate moderate-intensity statin with an aim towards achieving a 30 to 49% reduction in LDL-C concentration. If multiple ASCVD risk factors, age 50 to 77 years, initiate high-intensity aim to achieve over 50% reduction in LDL-C concentration. Without diabetes mellitus and 10-year ASCVD risk greater than 20% (high risk): Initiate high-intensity statin therapy with an aim towards achieving over 50% reduction in LDL-C concentration Without diabetes mellitus and 10-year ASCVD risk greater than or equal to 7.5% to less than 20% (intermediate risk): With the evaluation of risk enhancers (e.g., family history, chronic kidney disease, metabolic syndrome, inflammatory disease, ethnicity factors, condition-specific to women, persistently elevated LDL-C greater than or equal to 60 mg/dL) and coronary artery calcium score if uncertain. Initiate moderate-intensity statin with an aim towards achieving a 30 to 49% reduction in LDL-C concentration. Without diabetes mellitus and 10-year ASCVD risk 5 to less than 7.5% (borderline risk): Initiate lifestyle, select moderate intensity with the presence of risk enhancers (risk discussion indicated) Without diabetes mellitus and 10-year ASCVD risk less than 5% (low risk): Initiate lifestyle and risk discussion

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperlipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: All diabetes patients aged 40 to 75 should be started on moderate/high-intensity statin and titrated upwards based on tolerance, other risk factors, and LDL cholesterol levels. In diabetes patients without established ASCVD, however, with other risk factors, the LDL-C goal is < 70 mg/dl, and the statin dose should be titrated upwards to achieve that goal. In those with diabetes and established cardiovascular disease, the high-intensity statin should be used, ideally to lower LDL-C to <55 mg/dl. If LDL goals are not achieved with the highest dose of statin tolerated, an ezetimibe/PCSK-9 inhibitor should be added.

retrieved_docs.title: HMG-CoA Reductase Inhibitors -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary prevention (age over 18 years): History of multiple major ASCVD events (recent acute coronary syndrome within the past 12 months, history of MI, history of ischemic stroke, symptomatic peripheral artery disease) or one major ASCVD event with multiple high-risk conditions (age greater than or equal to 65 years, heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic kidney disease, current smoking, history of heart failure, history of PCI or CABG, persistently elevated LDL-C greater than or equal to 60 mg/dL). Very high-risk ASCVD: Initiate maximally tolerated statin. If LDL-C greater than or equal to 70 mg/dL add ezetimibe +/- PCSK9 inhibitor (with LDL-C greater than or equal to 70 mg/dL and non-HDL-C higher than 100 mg/dL) Stable ASCVD: Initiate high to moderate-intensity statin with the aim towards achieving LDL-C lowering as described above Additionally, statins are useful in the management of the following dyslipidemias:

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperlipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: For patients aged 40 to 75 years with LDL between 70 and 189 mg/dl AND without diabetes or clinical/established ASCVD, the ASCVD Risk Estimator Plus tool should be used, and patients should be risk-stratified. The stratification is as follows: Low risk (<5%), Borderline risk (5 to 7.4%), Intermediate risk (7.5 to 19.9%), and high risk (≥20%). The physician and patient should engage in a risk-benefit discussion and mutually agree on lifestyle modification and/or statin initiation. Patients with low risk will likely benefit from lifestyle modification only (Class I recommendation).

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Ischemic Heart Disease Selection of Treatment Modality -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The ASCVD pooled cohort equation was also used in the “2013 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Treatment of Blood Cholesterol to Reduce Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Risk in Adults” to risk stratify all adults over the age of 21. Patients with known ASCVD, LDL greater than 190mg/dL, diabetes mellitus or 10-year ASCVD calculated risk estimated at greater than 20% are considered high-risk. For this group of patients, high intensity (or maximally tolerated) statin therapy is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslipidemia -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: For secondary prevention, as defined by a patient with coronary artery disease, a target goal is set for LDL less than 70 mg/dL after being placed on a high-intensity statin for 6 weeks. If this goal is not met and LDL is significantly greater than 70, then combination therapy should be started in addition to high-intensity statin. The LDL goal should remain less than 70 mg/dL if the patient is not high-risk. If the patient is high risk (eg, had acute coronary syndrome in the last year, familial hypercholesterolemia, diabetes, chronic kidney disease stage 3 or 4, atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease event, or the need for revascularization while on a statin), the LDL goal is less than 50 mg/dL, and another agent is added.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Disease Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Statin use
retrieved_docs.content: A moderate-intensity statin is recommended to any patient aged between 40 to 75 years with type 2 DM, regardless of cholesterol levels and ASCVD risk. In this age group, for patients with low-density lipoprotein (LDL) exceeding 190, high or maximum tolerable intensity statin is recommended. In addition to diabetes and LDL values, the 10-year ASCVD risk should guide the use of statins. If the 10-year ASCVD risk is high (greater than 20%), the maximum tolerated statin should be used to reduce LDL by more than 50%. For intermediate-risk (7.5% to 20%), a moderate-intensity statin is recommended to reduce LDL by 30% or more. Discussion about initiating statin should begin with patients who are at borderline risk (5% to 7.5%), and there are risk enhancing factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperlipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: When discussing hyperlipidemia, the values of LDL-C and triglycerides matter the most, not the total cholesterol levels. Statins are always the first-line treatment for dyslipidemia because they confer the highest cardiovascular protection. Statins are classified as low intensity (<30% reduction of LDL from baseline), moderate-intensity (30 to 49%), and high intensity (>50%). Only two commercially available statins are high-intensity statins, atorvastatin 40 to 80 mg and rosuvastatin 20-40mg. The addition of ezetimibe, bile acid sequestrant, or PCSK-9 inhibitors to statins reduces LDL-C values by a further 15%, 25%, and 50%, respectively. [17] Ezetimibe is typically added to statins because of its safety and cost-efficacy.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Prevention -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Dyslipidemia - Ischemic stroke risk is increased by high cholesterol levels, whereas low cholesterol levels may increase the risk of hemorrhagic stroke. [5] Statin treatment reduces the risk of stroke in patients with or at high risk of atherosclerotic disease and even in healthy individuals with LDL < 130 mg/dL and high sensitivity CRP levels > 2mg/L. Despite initial concerns, statin treatment does not increase the risk of hemorrhagic CVA. Estimating total cardiovascular risk is the first step in the medical management of dyslipidemia. Clinical ASCVD, including stroke and TIA, is the major determinant of an individual’s CVD risk. LDL targets are based on risk factors or 10-year risk of ASCVD.  American college of endocrinology 2017 guidelines for management of dyslipidemia and prevention of cardiovascular disease recommend LDL target of < 55 mg/dL for extreme risk, < 70 mg/dL for very high risk, < 100 mg/dL for high and moderate risk and < 130 mg/dL for low-risk individuals. Fibrates are recommended for triglycerides >200 mg/dL and HDL < 40 mg/dL. Proprotein Convertase Subtilisin Kexin 9 (PCSK9) inhibitors are recommended for very high-risk individuals unable to reach LDL target of <140 mg/dL or < 100 mg/dL for those with multivessel or rapidly progressive atherosclerotic disease, despite statin therapy with or without ezetimibe therapy or because of inability to tolerate at least three statin medications. [5] A meta-analysis from Cholesterol Treatment Trialists' (CTT) Collaboration has shown that "in individuals with a 5-year risk of major vascular events lower than 10%, each one mmol/L reduction in LDL cholesterol produced an absolute reduction in major vascular events of about 11 per 1000 over 5 years." [9] For secondary prevention, SPARCL trial showed that the use of high-dose atorvastatin (80 mg) for cardioembolic stroke/TIA patients led to a 43% reduction in the risk of fatal recurrent stroke and a 26% reduction in any cardiovascular events. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Statin Medications -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate candidates for statin therapy based on established guidelines and individual patient risk factors. Screen lipid profile assessments and cardiovascular risk evaluations to determine the need for statin therapy in eligible patients. Select the most appropriate statin and dosage regimen based on patient preferences, comorbidities, and drug-drug interactions. Implement effective collaboration among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who benefit from statin medications. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Lipid-Lowering Drug Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Patients at an increased risk of cardiovascular events (cardiovascular death, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke, coronary revascularization, or unstable angina) benefit from lipid-lowering medications. It is essential to recognize that these drugs are useful in combination with lifestyle modification. These medications can be used for primary or secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. This activity covers lipid-lowering pharmaceutical interventions that members of the interprofessional team need to review the indications, contraindications, and adverse event profiles to optimally manage patients with abnormal lipid profiles and direct care to optimal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslipidemia -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: High-intensity statin therapy should start if the patient is aged 40 to 75, has LDL greater than or equal to 190 mg/dL, or has a history of diabetes and LDL between 70 to 189 mg/dL. Patients should be on a moderate or high-intensity statin if they are 40 to 75 years old, have LDL between 70 to 189 mg/dL, and have a 10-year ASCVD greater than or equal to 7.5%. The clinician-patient discussion should be undertaken to discuss the introduction of high or moderate-intensity statin in situations such as less than 5% to 7.5% 10-year ASCVD risk. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Cardiovascular and Diabetes Risks Based on National Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperlipidemia
retrieved_docs.content: For patients who have a clinical history of ASCVD and/or high risk for ASCVD who are already on statin therapy and LDL-C is well controlled, persistent hypertriglyceridemia (TG >135 mg/dl) should be treated with the addition of icosapent ethyl. Icosapent ethyl has been shown to reduce cardiovascular events in hypertriglyceridemic patients on statin therapy. [18] Adding niacin or fibrate to statin therapy does not seem to provide any benefit and may cause harm.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin and fluvastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, which leads to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As members of the same pharmacologic group, atorvastatin and fluvastatin share similar indications for treating hypercholesterolemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events, as well as comparable safety considerations such as the potential for myopathy and drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing both agents as part of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians apply class‑wide prescribing guidelines while selecting a specific statin based on potency, pharmacokinetic profile, and individual patient tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 779
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in cholesterol biosynthesis, which leads to reductions in low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and triglycerides and modest increases in high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are widely used to treat hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin belong to the same pharmacologic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, anticipating class‑related benefits, and monitoring for shared adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and chest pain (ألم الصدر) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). This relationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels can contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and may manifest as chest discomfort. Recognizing hypercholesterolemia as an underlying factor for these cardiac presentations underscores the importance of lipid screening and management in patients who exhibit chest pain or anginal symptoms, thereby aiding in risk stratification and preventive cardiovascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 848
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the therapeutic class known as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, a key step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and overall plasma lipid levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are commonly prescribed to reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with hypercholesterolemia, atherosclerotic disease, or other dyslipidemias, and they form a cornerstone of lipid‑lowering therapy in contemporary medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin and pravastatin are both classified as members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels in patients. As members of the same therapeutic category, fluvastatin and pravastatin share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hyperlipidemia and reduce cardiovascular risk. Recognizing that both agents belong to the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor, plain lipid‑modifying drug class helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual patient factors while understanding their shared pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Elevated blood cholesterol promotes atherosclerotic plaque development in coronary arteries, which can impair myocardial blood flow and present as chest pain. Recognizing this link enables clinicians to assess patients with chest pain for underlying lipid abnormalities and to consider lipid‑lowering interventions as part of comprehensive cardiovascular risk management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Lipid modifying agents are classified as a subset of cardiovascular system drugs, meaning they belong to the broader group of medications used to treat conditions of the cardiovascular system. As cardiovascular system drugs, lipid modifying agents are employed to manage abnormal lipid levels, lower cholesterol, and reduce the risk of atherosclerotic disease, thereby contributing to overall cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise a group of statin medications that share the same mechanism of action—blocking the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme to reduce cholesterol synthesis. The specific agents that belong to this class are fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. By lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, these statins are central to the management of hyperlipidaemia and the prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Understanding that fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑wide effects and potential adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2657
retrieved_docs.content: Rosuvastatin is classified as a member of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group, which are plain lipid‑modifying drugs used to lower cholesterol and manage dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 414
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin and pravastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, a group classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs that are used to lower cholesterol levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the drug class HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of medications works by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and improving lipid profiles. As members of this class, atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and pravastatin are commonly used to treat hyperlipidemia and to lower cardiovascular risk in patients requiring lipid management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.
